title: Encephalitis
authors: Karen L. Roos, Michael R. Wilson, Kenneth L. Tyler

items.title: Understanding Encephalitis: Definition and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Encephalitis is an inflammation of the brain primarily caused by viral infections or autoimmune processes. It often presents with acute febrile illness, altered consciousness, focal or diffuse neurological signs, seizures, and sometimes psychiatric symptoms.
keyPoints: Inflammation of the brain due to infection (usually viral) or autoimmune process
Commonly associated with meningitis and may involve spinal cord or nerve roots
Clinical manifestations include altered consciousness, neurologic disturbances like aphasia, ataxia, seizures, and hypothalamic-pituitary axis involvement
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Etiology of Encephalitis: Viral Causes in the U.S.
items.content: In the United States, herpesviruses such as HSV, VZV, and EBV are common causes of sporadic encephalitis in immunocompetent adults. Arboviruses like West Nile virus have caused significant epidemics, with WNV being responsible for most arbovirus neuroinvasive disease cases since 2002.
keyPoints: ~20,000 estimated annual cases of encephalitis in the U.S., likely underreported
Most common viruses: HSV, VZV, EBV
WNV caused 28,684 confirmed neuroinvasive disease cases from 1999–2022 with 2641 deaths
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Emerging Viral Causes of Encephalitis and Neurologic Disorders
items.content: New viral causes of encephalitis are emerging globally, including Nipah virus in Southeast Asia, Toscana virus in Europe, Zika virus in South America, and chikungunya virus in Africa and India. Dengue virus cases are rising in the Caribbean and Puerto Rico.
keyPoints: Emerging viruses causing encephalitis: Nipah, Toscana, Zika, chikungunya
Dengue virus cases increasing in the Caribbean and Puerto Rico
Parechoviruses cause fever, sepsis, and meningitis in infants under 3 months
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: CSF Examination in Suspected Viral Encephalitis: Indications and Interpretation
items.content: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) examination is crucial for diagnosing viral encephalitis, except when contraindicated by severely increased intracranial pressure. Collect at least 20 mL of CSF initially, storing 5-10 mL frozen for further tests like PCR or metagenomic sequencing due to the transient presence of neuroinvasive viruses in CSF.
keyPoints: CSF pleocytosis (>5 cells/μL) occurs in >95% of immunocompetent patients with viral encephalitis.
Severely immunocompromised patients may not show a CSF inflammatory response.
Cell counts >500/μL occur in ~10% of encephalitis cases; >1000/μL suggests nonviral infections or other processes.
Polymorphonuclear pleocytosis (~45%) is common in WNV and CMV myeloradiculitis in immunocompromised patients.
CSF red blood cells (>500/μL) can be seen in ~20% of encephalitis cases, often due to microhemorrhages.
Decreased CSF glucose concentration is unusual for viral encephalitis and suggests other infections or conditions.

items.title: Interpreting Atypical Findings in CSF Analysis for Encephalitis
items.content: Atypical lymphocytes may indicate EBV infection, while increased plasmacytoid or Mollaret-like cells suggest WNV encephalitis. Persistent CSF neutrophilia should raise suspicion of bacterial infection or other noninfectious processes.
keyPoints: Atypical lymphocytes in CSF are seen with EBV and less commonly with CMV, HSV, enteroviruses.
Plasmacytoid or Mollaret-like cells reported in WNV encephalitis.
Polymorphonuclear pleocytosis (~45%) occurs in WNV encephalitis; large numbers suggest EEE virus, echovirus 9, and other enteroviruses.
Persisting CSF neutrophilia should prompt consideration of bacterial infection or noninfectious processes.
Decreased CSF glucose concentration is rare but may occur with mumps, LCMV, VZV, advanced HSV encephalitis, and CMV myeloradiculitis.

items.title: Interpreting HSV CSF PCR Results
items.content: HSV CSF PCR is a critical diagnostic tool with high sensitivity (~96%) and specificity (~99%). However, interpretation requires consideration of disease likelihood, timing relative to symptom onset, and prior antiviral therapy. A negative test significantly reduces but does not eliminate the probability of HSV encephalitis.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of HSV CSF PCR: ~96%
Specificity of HSV CSF PCR: ~99%
Negative HSV CSF PCR in a patient with 35% pretest probability reduces posttest probability to ~2%
Negative HSV CSF PCR in a patient with 60% pretest probability reduces posttest probability to ~6%
Positive test makes diagnosis almost certain (98–99%)
Frequency of positive HSV CSF PCRs decreases over time, with only ~20% remaining positive after ≥14 days

items.title: Sensitivity and Specificity of Enteroviral CSF RT-PCR
items.content: Enteroviral CSF RT-PCR generally has high sensitivity and specificity (>95%), but varies for specific enteroviruses. EV-A71 shows lower sensitivity (~30%). Patients with EV-D68-associated AFM rarely have positive CSF RT-PCR (<3%) but may test positive on nasopharyngeal swabs.
keyPoints: Enteroviral CSF RT-PCR sensitivity and specificity: >95%
EV-A71 CSF RT-PCR sensitivity: ~30% in some reports
<3% of patients with EV-D68-associated AFM have positive CSF RT-PCR

items.title: SARS-CoV-2 and Encephalitis Diagnosis
items.content: In SARS-CoV-2 cases, nasopharyngeal RT-PCR is positive for both encephalopathy and encephalitis. However, only true encephalitis cases show a positive CSF RT-PCR for SARS-CoV-2, indicating viral CNS invasion.
keyPoints: Nasopharyngeal RT-PCR positive in both encephalopathy and encephalitis due to SARS-CoV-2
Positive CSF RT-PCR for SARS-CoV-2 indicates true encephalitis

items.title: Diagnostic Utility of CSF WNV IgM for Encephalitis
items.content: CSF WNV IgM antibodies are diagnostic of West Nile Virus encephalitis due to their restricted passage across the blood-brain barrier, indicating intrathecal synthesis.
keyPoints: Presence of CSF WNV IgM is indicative of intrathecal synthesis and thus diagnostic of WNV encephalitis.
CSF WNV IgM seropositivity increases during the first week after illness onset, reaching 80% or higher by day 7.
Serum and CSF IgM antibodies generally persist for a few months, but exceptions exist with serum IgM lasting >1 year in some cases.

items.title: Role of HSV CSF Antibody Testing
items.content: HSV CSF antibody testing is valuable for patients with prolonged illness (>1 week) and negative CSF PCR, especially when considering VZV infection where both CSF IgM antibodies and PCR should be used complementarily.
keyPoints: Optimal detection of HSV antibodies and antigens in CSF occurs after the first week of illness.
HSV CSF antibody testing is useful for patients with illness >1 week duration who are CSF PCR negative for HSV.
For VZV, both CSF IgM antibody tests and PCR should be considered complementary.

items.title: Imaging and EEG in Encephalitis Diagnosis
items.content: MRI, CT, and EEG are crucial for differentiating encephalitic processes and identifying focal findings suggestive of HSV encephalitis.
keyPoints: MRI is more sensitive than CT; 10% of PCR-documented HSV cases may have normal MRI, but 80% show asymmetric temporal lobe abnormalities.
CT is less sensitive, with up to 35% of patients showing normal results.
EEG abnormalities occur in >75% of PCR-documented HSV encephalitis cases, often involving the temporal lobes.

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.title: MRI Findings in WNV vs. HSV Encephalitis
items.content: In patients with West Nile Virus (WNV) encephalitis, significant MRI abnormalities are found in approximately two-thirds of cases, which is less frequent than in Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) encephalitis. When present, these abnormalities typically involve deep brain structures such as the thalamus, basal ganglia, and brainstem rather than the cortex.
keyPoints: Approximately two-thirds of WNV encephalitis patients show significant MRI abnormalities.
MRI abnormalities in WNV often involve deep brain structures (thalamus, basal ganglia, brainstem).
WNV MRI patterns can resemble those seen with other arboviruses like Japanese and St. Louis encephalitis viruses.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.title: Indications for Brain Biopsy in Encephalitis
items.content: Brain biopsy is generally reserved for patients with encephalitis when CSF PCR studies do not lead to a specific diagnosis, and there are focal abnormalities on MRI. It is considered if there's no serologic evidence of autoimmune disease and the patient continues to deteriorate clinically despite treatment with acyclovir and supportive therapy.
keyPoints: Brain biopsy indicated when CSF PCR fails to diagnose.
Considered in patients with focal MRI abnormalities.
No serologic evidence of autoimmune disease should be present.
Clinical deterioration persists despite acyclovir and supportive therapy.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.title: Autoimmune Encephalitis Mimics Viral Infection
items.content: Autoimmune encephalitis can mimic viral infections, particularly HSV encephalitis. This is due to the presence of specific antibodies like NMDA receptor antibodies, which have been reported in up to 25% of patients recovering from HSV encephalitis.
keyPoints: NMDA receptor antibodies found in up to 25% of post-HSV encephalitis recovery cases
Autoimmune encephalitis can be paraneoplastic or associated with onconneuronal antibodies (e.g., anti-Hu, Yo, Ma2)
Diagnosis by detection of specific autoantibodies in serum and/or CSF

items.title: Amebic Encephalitis: Naegleria fowleri
items.content: Naegleria fowleri causes acute meningoencephalitis, often in immunocompetent children with a history of swimming in warm water. CSF resembles bacterial meningitis with neutrophilic pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia.
keyPoints: Infection occurs in warm, iron-rich pools
CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia
Mortality approaches 100%, with few recoveries reported using nitroxoline therapy

items.title: Distinguishing HSV from Other Viral Encephalitis
items.content: HSV encephalitis requires early antiviral treatment, unlike other viral causes where therapy is supportive. Diagnosis involves CSF PCR analysis for HSV, with a positive result confirming the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Early antiviral therapy is crucial for HSV encephalitis
CSF PCR for HSV is diagnostic; a negative test reduces likelihood of HSV
Clinical features include inferomedial frontotemporal involvement and focal findings

items.title: Recognizing Rabies: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Rabies can present with different clinical features depending on the source of infection. Classic rabies from a dog or wolf bite often presents with fever, fluctuating consciousness, autonomic hyperactivity, hydrophobia, and aerophobia. In contrast, bat-bite rabies may manifest as focal neurologic deficits, myoclonus, seizures, and hallucinations without phobic spasms. Diagnosis involves detecting rabies virus antigen in brain tissue or neural innervation at the nape of the neck, with PCR amplification from CSF, saliva, or tears being useful.
keyPoints: Classic rabies presents with fever, fluctuating consciousness, autonomic hyperactivity, hydrophobia, and aerophobia.
Bat-bite rabies may present with focal neurologic deficits, myoclonus, seizures, and hallucinations; phobic spasms are not typical.
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis and increased T2 signal in brainstem, hippocampus, hypothalamus may be seen.
Diagnosis by finding rabies virus antigen in brain tissue or neural innervation at the nape of the neck.
PCR amplification from CSF, saliva, or tears can aid diagnosis.
Serology is often negative in the first week after onset.

items.title: Epidemiologic Clues and Public Health Resources for Encephalitis
items.content: Epidemiologic factors such as season, geographic location, travel history, and exposure to animals or vectors are crucial in diagnosing viral encephalitis. Rabies transmission from bats is a significant risk factor in the U.S., despite fewer cases from dogs. Public health authorities provide updates on arboviral encephalitis cases, aiding in identifying regional agents.
keyPoints: Consider season, geographic location, travel history, and exposure to animals or vectors for diagnosis.
In the U.S., bat bites are a common rabies risk factor; dog bites less so.
Public health updates on arboviral encephalitis available via CDC and USGS websites.

items.title: Empirical Acyclovir Use in Suspected Viral Encephalitis
items.content: Acyclovir should be started empirically for patients with suspected viral encephalitis, particularly if focal features are present. It is beneficial against HSV and should continue unless HSV is ruled out, except possibly in severe cases of VZV or EBV.
keyPoints: Empirical treatment with acyclovir is recommended for suspected viral encephalitis with focal features.
Acyclovir dosage: 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (30 mg/kg/day) for 21 days in adults; 20 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (60 mg/kg/day) for at least 21 days in neonates.
Discontinue acyclovir if HSV is ruled out, except possibly in severe VZV or EBV cases.

items.title: Acyclovir Administration and Monitoring
items.content: Acyclovir should be diluted to ≤7 mg/mL before IV administration, infused over 1 hour to minimize renal dysfunction risk. Monitor for complications such as renal dysfunction, thrombocytopenia, gastrointestinal toxicity, and neurotoxicity.
keyPoints: Dilute acyclovir to a concentration of ≤7 mg/mL.
Infuse each dose slowly over 1 hour to minimize renal dysfunction risk (9% incidence).
Monitor for complications: renal dysfunction (5%), thrombocytopenia (6%), gastrointestinal toxicity (7%), neurotoxicity (1%).

items.title: Considerations for Adjunctive Therapies in Viral Encephalitis
items.content: The role of adjunctive intravenous glucocorticoids remains unclear, with no data from randomized controlled trials. Oral antivirals post-IV acyclovir do not improve outcomes in HSV encephalitis.
keyPoints: Adjunctive intravenous glucocorticoids' efficacy is uncertain; no RCT data available.
Oral antivirals like valaciclovir after IV acyclovir do not enhance outcomes in adult HSV encephalitis.

items.title: Ganciclovir Dosage and Monitoring in CMV Neurologic Illness
items.content: Ganciclovir is used for treating severe neurologic illnesses related to CMV, administered intravenously at a dose of 5 mg/kg every 12 hours. Induction therapy continues until there's a decline in CSF pleocytosis and reduction in CSF CMV DNA copy number. Maintenance therapy follows daily dosing.
keyPoints: Ganciclovir triphosphate inhibits CMV DNA polymerase, causing premature chain termination.
CSF concentrations are 25–70% of plasma levels post-intravenous administration.
Induction therapy continues until decline in CSF pleocytosis and reduction in CSF CMV DNA copy number.
Maintenance dose: 5 mg/kg daily.
Adjust doses for renal insufficiency; monitor for granulocytopenia (20–25%) and thrombocytopenia.

items.title: Foscarnet Use in CMV Neurologic Illness
items.content: Foscarnet, a pyrophosphate analogue, is used for serious CMV-related neurologic illnesses. Administered intravenously at 60 mg/kg every 8 hours during induction therapy (14–21 days), followed by maintenance therapy.
keyPoints: Inhibits viral DNA polymerases by binding to the pyrophosphate-binding site.
CSF concentrations range from 15 to 100% of plasma levels post-infusion.
Induction dose: 60 mg/kg every 8 hours; maintenance: 60–120 mg/kg per day.
Extend induction if no decline in CSF pleocytosis and CMV DNA copy number.
Monitor for renal impairment (1/3 of patients), fatigue, nausea, and electrolyte disturbances (~15%).

items.title: Cidofovir's Role in CMV Retinitis and CNS Disease
items.content: Cidofovir is used for treating CMV retinitis, with limited data on efficacy in human CMV CNS disease. Administered intravenously at 5 mg/kg once weekly after prehydration and probenecid co-administration.
keyPoints: Nucleotide analogue effective against CMV retinitis; potential for murine CMV encephalitis.
Dose: 5 mg/kg IV once weekly, then biweekly based on response.
Prehydrate with normal saline and use probenecid to mitigate nephrotoxicity.
Nephrotoxicity is common; adjust dose if renal function deteriorates.

items.title: Sequelae of Eastern Equine Encephalitis Virus Infection
items.content: Eastern equine encephalitis virus infection results in severe neurologic sequelae for the majority of survivors.
keyPoints: Nearly 80% of survivors have severe neurologic sequelae.
This highlights the critical need for early intervention and supportive care to potentially mitigate long-term impacts.

items.title: Sequelae in Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) Encephalitis Treated with Acyclovir
items.content: The severity of sequelae in HSV encephalitis treated with acyclovir is related to the patient's age and initial level of consciousness.
keyPoints: Of 32 acyclovir-treated patients, 26 survived (81%).
12 survivors had no or only minor sequelae (46%), while 11 required continuous supportive care due to severe impairment (42%).
Younger patients (<30 years) with good neurologic function at initiation of therapy had better outcomes: 100% survival and 62% with no or mild sequelae.
Older patients (>30 years) showed a lower survival rate (64%) and fewer without or with mild sequelae (57%).
Patients with severe impairment (Glasgow Coma Scale score 6) at therapy initiation either died or survived with severe sequelae.

items.title: Risk Factors for Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy
items.content: PML is primarily associated with immunosuppressive disorders or therapies. The highest risk of PML is linked to natalizumab, especially in patients who are JCV seropositive and have prior immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: Almost all PML cases involve an underlying immunosuppressive condition or therapy.
AIDS is the most common disorder associated with PML.
Natalizumab has a high risk of PML, estimated at ~4 cases per 1000 treated patients.
Risk factors include JCV serostatus, prior immunosuppressive therapy, and duration of natalizumab therapy (>24 months).
Extended dosing intervals for natalizumab may reduce the risk.

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy
items.content: Patients with PML often present with visual deficits, mental impairment, weakness, ataxia, and seizures. The clinical features are similar in HIV-associated PML and immunomodulatory drug-associated PML.
keyPoints: Visual deficits occur in 45% of patients, typically as homonymous hemianopia.
Mental impairment is seen in 38% of cases (dementia, confusion, personality change).
Weakness includes hemi- or monoparesis; ataxia is also common.
Seizures occur in ~20% of patients, especially with cortical lesions.

items.title: Diagnostic Imaging and Features of Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy
items.content: MRI is crucial for suggesting PML diagnosis, showing characteristic lesions. HIV-PML lesions are typically nonenhancing, while immunomodulatory drug-associated PML may show enhancement.
keyPoints: MRI reveals multifocal asymmetric white matter lesions with increased T2/FLAIR and decreased T1 signal.
Lesions are located periventricularly, in the centrum semiovale, parietal-occipital region, and cerebellum.
HIV-PML lesions are nonenhancing (90%), but immunomodulatory drug-associated PML may show peripheral ring enhancement.
CT scans show hypodense nonenhancing white matter lesions.

items.title: Diagnostic Value and Limitations of CSF PCR for JCV DNA in PML
items.content: CSF PCR amplification for JCV DNA is a critical diagnostic tool for Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML), especially when combined with typical MRI findings. The assay's specificity ranges from 92–100%, making it highly reliable when positive. However, its sensitivity varies: approximately 70-90% in HIV-negative and untreated HIV-positive patients, but drops to about 60% in ART-treated individuals due to lower JCV viral loads. In cases of natalizumab-associated PML, variability in JCV DNA levels can lead to negative results in standard commercial assays with detection thresholds >100 copies/μL, though supersensitive techniques detecting as low as 10 copies/μL may yield positive results. Low JCV loads (<100 copies/μL) generally indicate a better prognosis.
keyPoints: CSF PCR for JCV DNA is highly specific (92–100%) but has variable sensitivity: ~70-90% in HIV-negative and untreated HIV-positive patients, ~60% in ART-treated patients.
Commercial assays typically detect >100 copies/μL; reference labs may use supersensitive techniques detecting 10 copies/μL or less.
Low JCV loads (<100 copies/μL) correlate with a better prognosis.
Negative CSF PCR does not exclude PML, and brain biopsy might be necessary for definitive diagnosis.

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.title: Therapeutic Strategies for Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML)
items.content: In PML, the primary therapeutic strategy involves enhancing or restoring immunocompetence due to its occurrence in immunocompromised individuals. While no consistently effective therapy is available, interventions like ART have shown benefits by improving immune status.
keyPoints: No consistently effective therapy for PML is available.
Potential beneficial effects of mirtazapine and interferon-α reported in noncontrolled studies.
Mefloquine, cytarabine, and cidofovir failed to show significant benefit in controlled trials.
Pembrolizumab demonstrated clinical improvement in small series.
BK or JC virus-specific cytotoxic T lymphocytes showed positive results in case series.
ART improves 1-year survival (~50%) but may lead to IRIS, treated with intravenous glucocorticoids.
Higher CD4 counts (>300/μL) and low viral loads correlate with better prognosis.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.title: Epidemiology and Prognosis of Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis (SSPE)
items.content: SSPE is a rare, chronic demyelinating disease linked to measles virus infection. Its incidence has declined with vaccination but may rise due to vaccine hesitancy.
keyPoints: SSPE is associated with chronic non-permissive measles virus infection in the CNS.
Frequency estimated at 1 in 100,000–500,000 measles cases.
Average of five cases per year reported in the United States.
Incidence declined post-measles vaccine introduction but may rise due to increasing vaccine hesitancy.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.question: Which virus is most commonly responsible for arbovirus encephalitis cases in the United States since 2002?
items.explanation: Since 2002, West Nile virus has been responsible for the majority of arbovirus meningitis and encephalitis cases in the United States.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is NOT typically associated with encephalitis?
items.explanation: Chronic cough is not a typical clinical manifestation of encephalitis.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is the estimated number of encephalitis cases per year in the United States?
items.explanation: There are an estimated ~20,000 cases of encephalitis per year in the United States.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which virus is NOT listed as a common cause of sporadic acute encephalitis in immunocompetent adults?
items.explanation: Influenza virus is not listed as a common cause of sporadic acute encephalitis in immunocompetent adults.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: In the context of viral encephalitis, which characteristic is typically observed in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) examination?
options: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose concentration
Polymorphonuclear pleocytosis with elevated protein concentration
items.explanation: The typical CSF profile in viral encephalitis includes lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein concentration, and a normal glucose concentration.

items.question: Which patient population may fail to mount an inflammatory response in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) during viral encephalitis?
items.explanation: Severely immunocompromised patients, such as those with HIV infection or on immunosuppressant drugs, may not exhibit a CSF inflammatory response.

items.question: What should be considered if there is persisting polymorphonuclear pleocytosis in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of a patient with encephalitis?
options: Noninfectious processes like acute hemorrhagic leukoencephalitis
Bacterial infection or other specific infections
items.explanation: Persisting CSF polymorphonuclear pleocytosis should prompt consideration of bacterial infection, leptospirosis, amebic infection, and noninfectious processes.

items.question: Which virus has a CSF PCR sensitivity of approximately 96% and specificity of approximately 99% for CNS infections?
items.explanation: The passage states that HSV CSF PCR has a sensitivity (~96%) and specificity (~99%).

items.question: What is the approximate reduction in posttest probability of HSV encephalitis when an initial pretest probability is 60% and a negative HSV CSF PCR test result is obtained?
items.explanation: A patient with a pretest probability of 60% has their posttest probability reduced to ~6% after a negative HSV CSF PCR.

items.question: What is the sensitivity of enteroviral (EV) CSF RT-PCR for EV-A71 as reported in some studies?
items.explanation: The passage notes that EV RT-PCR sensitivity for EV-A71 may be considerably lower (~30%) in some reports.

items.question: Which test is generally less sensitive than the detection of WNV-specific CSF IgM in patients with West Nile virus infection?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that CSF PCR is less sensitive than detection of WNV-specific CSF IgM for West Nile virus infection.

items.question: In patients with suspected WNV encephalitis, what is the significance of detecting CSF IgM antibodies?
options: It indicates a systemic infection rather than CNS involvement.
It suggests intrathecal synthesis and supports a diagnosis of WNV encephalitis.
It confirms that the patient has been previously infected with WNV.
It is not useful because CSF IgM antibodies are typically absent in viral infections.
items.explanation: CSF WNV IgM antibodies indicate intrathecal synthesis and support a diagnosis of WNV encephalitis due to restricted passage through the blood-brain barrier.

items.question: Why is HSV CSF antibody testing valuable in certain patients?
options: It provides immediate results for acute diagnosis.
It can be useful when the illness duration exceeds one week and PCR is negative.
It replaces the need for neuroimaging studies.
It is always positive in all cases of HSV encephalitis.
items.explanation: HSV CSF antibody testing is valuable in patients whose illness is >1 week and who are PCR negative, as it aids in diagnosis when PCR results are not definitive.

items.question: What does the presence of CSF IgM antibodies indicate for viruses with high seroprevalence like VZV and HSV?
options: It confirms a systemic infection without CNS involvement.
It suggests intrathecal synthesis, indicating possible CNS infection.
It is irrelevant due to high background seroprevalence rates.
It indicates that the patient has never been exposed to these viruses.
items.explanation: For VZV and HSV, CSF IgM antibodies suggest intrathecal synthesis, providing presumptive evidence of CNS infection despite high seroprevalence rates.

items.question: When is optimal detection of both HSV antibodies and antigens in CSF typically achieved?
options: Immediately after the onset of symptoms.
During the first week of illness.
After the first week of illness.
items.explanation: Optimal detection of HSV antibodies and antigens in CSF typically occurs after the first week of illness, limiting their utility for acute diagnosis.

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.question: Which virus is most likely to show MRI abnormalities involving deep brain structures such as the thalamus, basal ganglia, and brainstem?
items.explanation: MRI abnormalities involving deep brain structures are more characteristic of West Nile Virus encephalitis.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.question: Which EEG pattern is most commonly associated with WNV encephalitis?
options: Temporally predominant sharp or periodic discharges
Multifocal areas of hemorrhagic and ischemic infarction
Enlarged ventricles with increased T2 signal
items.explanation: EEGs in patients with WNV encephalitis typically show generalized slowing that may be more anteriorly prominent.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.question: In which condition is brain biopsy generally reserved for when CSF PCR studies fail to lead to a specific diagnosis?
options: Any encephalitis with focal MRI abnormalities and progressive clinical deterioration
items.explanation: Brain biopsy is reserved for patients in whom CSF PCR studies fail to lead to a specific diagnosis, who have focal abnormalities on MRI, no serologic evidence of autoimmune disease, and continue to show progressive clinical deterioration despite treatment.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.question: Which organism is associated with acute meningoencephalitis that mimics bacterial meningitis in its CSF profile?
items.explanation: Naegleria fowleri causes acute meningoencephalitis with a CSF profile resembling bacterial meningitis.

items.question: What is the recommended diagnostic procedure to confirm HSV encephalitis?
items.explanation: CSF PCR analysis for HSV is the diagnostic procedure of choice to confirm HSV encephalitis.

items.question: Which autoantibody is associated with new or worsening symptoms following recovery from HSV encephalitis?
items.explanation: NMDA receptor antibodies have been reported in patients recovering from HSV encephalitis, potentially causing new or worsening symptoms.

items.question: Which clinical feature should raise suspicion for HSV encephalitis?
items.explanation: HSV encephalitis should be considered with clinical features like prominent olfactory or gustatory hallucinations.

items.question: Which of the following is a common risk factor for rabies in the United States?
items.explanation: In the U.S., exposure to bats is a more common risk factor for rabies than dog bites.

items.question: What clinical feature distinguishes paralytic rabies from encephalitic rabies?
items.explanation: Paralytic rabies is characterized by acute ascending paralysis, unlike encephalitic rabies.

items.question: Which diagnostic method can be used to identify rabies virus antigen in patients suspected of having rabies?
options: Finding rabies virus antigen in brain tissue
items.explanation: Rabies diagnosis can be confirmed by finding rabies virus antigen in brain tissue.

items.question: Which enzyme is responsible for phosphorylating acyclovir to produce acyclovir-5'-monophosphate in HSV, VZV, and EBV infections?
items.explanation: HSV, VZV, and EBV encode an enzyme called deoxypyrimidine (thymidine) kinase that phosphorylates acyclovir.

items.question: What is the recommended intravenous dose of acyclovir for adults with suspected viral encephalitis?
items.explanation: Adults should receive a dose of 10 mg/kg intravenously every 8 hours for suspected viral encephalitis.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a complication associated with acyclovir therapy?
items.explanation: Complications of acyclovir include renal dysfunction, thrombocytopenia, and gastrointestinal toxicity, but not hepatotoxicity.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which acyclovir exerts its antiviral effect?
options: Inhibition of viral DNA polymerase and premature termination of viral DNA chains
Blocking viral entry into host cells
items.explanation: Acyclovir acts as an antiviral agent by inhibiting viral DNA polymerase and causing premature termination of nascent viral DNA chains.

items.question: Which antiviral drug acts as a competitive inhibitor of CMV DNA polymerase by incorporating into nascent viral DNA, resulting in premature chain termination?
items.explanation: Ganciclovir acts as a competitive inhibitor of CMV DNA polymerase by incorporating into viral DNA, causing premature chain termination.

items.question: What is the usual induction dose for ganciclovir in treating severe neurologic illnesses?
items.explanation: The usual induction dose for ganciclovir is 5 mg/kg every 12 hours intravenously.

items.question: Which antiviral drug requires prehydration with normal saline and probenecid administration to mitigate nephrotoxicity?
items.explanation: Cidofovir requires prehydration with normal saline and probenecid to reduce nephrotoxicity.

items.question: What is a common side effect of foscarnet treatment that may be associated with cardiac rhythm disturbances?
items.explanation: Foscarnet often causes renal impairment, which can lead to cardiac rhythm disturbances.

items.question: What percentage of survivors from EEE virus infection have severe neurologic sequelae?
items.explanation: Nearly 80% of survivors from EEE virus infection have severe neurologic sequelae.

items.question: What proportion of acyclovir-treated HSV encephalitis patients had no or only minor sequelae?
items.explanation: Of the survivors, 46% had no or only minor sequelae.

items.question: Which factor is directly related to the incidence and severity of sequelae in viral encephalitis?
options: The level of consciousness at initiation of therapy
The type of virus causing the infection
items.explanation: The incidence and severity of sequelae were directly related to the age of the patient and the level of consciousness at the time of initiation of therapy.

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic cytologic alteration in astrocytes seen in PML?
options: Astrocytes are reduced in size with hyperchromatic nuclei.
Astrocytes are enlarged and contain hyperchromatic, deformed, and bizarre nuclei.
Astrocytes have densely staining nuclei that contain JC virus particles.
Astrocytes show no significant cytologic changes.
items.explanation: Astrocytes in PML are enlarged with hyperchromatic, deformed, and bizarre nuclei.

items.question: Which immunosuppressive disorder is most commonly associated with PML?
items.explanation: The most common immunosuppressive disorder associated with PML is AIDS.

items.question: What is the estimated risk of developing PML in patients treated with natalizumab?
items.explanation: The overall risk of PML in natalizumab-treated patients is estimated at ~4 cases per 1000.

items.question: Which imaging feature distinguishes immunomodulatory drug-associated PML from HIV-PML?
options: Increased likelihood of MRI enhancement of lesions
Lesions are typically associated with edema
Decreased signal on T2 and FLAIR images
items.explanation: Immunomodulatory drug-associated PML has an increased likelihood of MRI enhancement of lesions.

items.question: Which diagnostic tool has a high specificity for PML when positive in the appropriate clinical setting?
options: PCR amplification of JCV DNA from CSF
items.explanation: PCR amplification of JCV DNA from CSF has a high specificity (92–100%) for diagnosing PML.

items.question: In ART-treated patients, what is the approximate sensitivity of JCV DNA PCR in CSF?
items.explanation: Sensitivity may be closer to 60% in ART-treated patients due to lower JCV viral loads.

items.question: What does a negative CSF PCR for JCV DNA indicate about the diagnosis of PML?
options: It confirms the absence of PML.
It excludes the diagnosis of PML.
It does not exclude the diagnosis of PML.
items.explanation: A negative CSF PCR for JCV DNA does not exclude the diagnosis of PML.

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.question: Which agent has been suggested to inhibit the binding of JCV to its receptor on oligodendrocytes in PML?
items.explanation: Mirtazapine, a 5-HT2a receptor antagonist, may inhibit JCV binding to its receptor on oligodendrocytes.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.question: Which treatment has been associated with clinical improvement in HIV-positive PML patients?
items.explanation: Pembrolizumab, a PD-1 inhibitor, has shown clinical improvement and stabilization in HIV-positive PML patients.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.question: What is the approximate 1-year survival rate for HIV-positive PML patients treated with ART?
items.explanation: The 1-year survival rate for HIV-positive PML patients treated with ART is approximately 50%.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.statement: Most cases of acute encephalitis with a suspected viral etiology remain of unknown cause.
items.explanation: Despite comprehensive diagnostic efforts, most cases of acute encephalitis with a suspected viral etiology remain of unknown cause.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Epidemics of encephalitis are caused by bacteria.
items.explanation: Epidemics of encephalitis are caused by arboviruses, not bacteria.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: A decreased CSF glucose concentration is a common finding in viral encephalitis.
items.explanation: Decreased CSF glucose concentration is distinctly unusual in viral encephalitis and suggests other types of infections.

items.statement: CSF pleocytosis occurs in more than 95% of immunocompetent patients with documented viral encephalitis.
items.explanation: A CSF pleocytosis (>5 cells/μL) is observed in over 95% of immunocompetent patients with viral encephalitis.

items.statement: A negative HSV CSF PCR test performed at the appropriate time excludes HSV encephalitis.
items.explanation: The passage states that a negative HSV CSF PCR significantly reduces but does not exclude the likelihood of HSV encephalitis.

items.statement: Unbiased metagenomic sequencing technologies have shown promise for diagnosing obscure cases of encephalitis.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that unbiased metagenomic sequencing has great potential for rapid diagnosis in complex encephalitis cases.

items.statement: Serologic studies are most useful for acute diagnosis and management in viral encephalitis.
items.explanation: Serologic data are mainly used for retrospective diagnosis due to the delay between infection onset and antibody response.

items.statement: MRI findings in HSV encephalitis can be normal in up to 10% of PCR-documented cases.
items.explanation: Approximately 10% of patients with PCR-documented HSV encephalitis may have a normal MRI.

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.statement: EEGs in patients with HSV encephalitis typically show generalized slowing.
items.explanation: EEGs in HSV encephalitis are more characterized by temporally predominant sharp or periodic discharges.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.statement: Prominent cerebellar T2/FLAIR abnormalities can be observed with Powassan virus encephalitis.
items.explanation: Prominent cerebellar T2/FLAIR abnormalities have been observed with Powassan virus encephalitis.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.statement: A positive CSF PCR test for HSV excludes the diagnosis of HSV encephalitis.
items.explanation: A positive CSF PCR test establishes the diagnosis of HSV encephalitis.

items.statement: Balamuthia mandrillaris infection has a mortality rate approaching 100%.
items.explanation: No effective treatment for Balamuthia mandrillaris amebic encephalitis has been approved, and mortality approaches 100%.

items.statement: Serology is a reliable method for the acute diagnosis of rabies.
items.explanation: Serology is frequently negative in both serum and CSF in the first week after onset, limiting its acute diagnostic utility.

items.statement: Patients with rabies can show areas of increased T2 signal abnormality in the brainstem.
items.explanation: Rabies patients may exhibit increased T2 signal abnormalities in the brainstem, among other regions.

items.statement: Oral acyclovir is recommended as primary therapy for encephalitis.
items.explanation: Oral antiviral drugs, including acyclovir, have not been evaluated in the treatment of encephalitis as primary therapy.

items.statement: Acyclovir resistance is a significant clinical problem in immunocompetent individuals.
items.explanation: To date, acyclovir-resistant isolates have not been a significant clinical problem in immunocompetent individuals.

items.statement: Ribavirin is the first-line treatment for West Nile virus encephalitis.
items.explanation: No specific antiviral therapy of proven efficacy exists for West Nile virus encephalitis; ribavirin has been used but lacks clinical trial support.

items.statement: Ganciclovir is administered orally in its active form.
items.explanation: Valganciclovir, not ganciclovir itself, is the orally bioavailable prodrug that generates high serum levels of ganciclovir.

items.statement: Severe sequelae are common in infections due to EBV, California encephalitis virus, and Venezuelan equine encephalitis virus.
items.explanation: Severe sequelae are unusual in infections due to these viruses.

items.statement: Patients with severe neurologic impairment at initiation of therapy either died or survived with severe sequelae.
items.explanation: Patients with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 6 at initiation of therapy either died or survived with severe sequelae.

items.statement: PML typically involves the spinal cord and optic nerves.
items.explanation: PML spares the spinal cord and optic nerves.

items.statement: Seizures occur in approximately 20% of PML patients.
items.explanation: Seizures occur in ~20% of PML patients, predominantly those with cortical lesions.

items.statement: Pleocytosis is a common finding in CSF for patients with PML.
items.explanation: Pleocytosis occurs in less than 25% of cases and rarely exceeds 25 cells/μL.

items.statement: Serologic studies of JCV antibody are highly valuable for diagnosing PML.
items.explanation: Serologic studies have modest value due to high basal seroprevalence levels.

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.statement: SSPE incidence has increased due to rising measles cases.
items.explanation: The incidence of SSPE may rise with increasing measles cases due to vaccine hesitancy.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.subtopic: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS
items.statement: Cidofovir has shown significant benefit in HIV-associated PML.
items.explanation: A randomized controlled trial of cidofovir in HIV-associated PML failed to show significant benefit.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy | SUBACUTE SCLEROSING PANENCEPHALITIS

items.assertion: The majority of cases of West Nile virus neuroinvasive disease occur in August and September.
items.reason: West Nile virus epidemics are unpredictable, but most cases do occur during these months.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Herpes simplex virus is a common cause of encephalitis in immunocompromised adults.
items.reason: Herpes simplex virus is listed as one of the most commonly identified viruses causing sporadic cases of acute encephalitis in immunocompetent adults.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because it refers to immunocompetent adults.
items.reference: Encephalitis · Encephalitis | DEFINITION | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Positive EBV CSF PCR results may reflect reactivation of latent EBV due to unrelated infections.
items.reason: EBV CSF PCR specificity has not been established, and positive tests can occur with other pathogens present.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why positive EBV CSF PCR results may be seen.

items.assertion: In patients with SARS-CoV-2 encephalitis, nasopharyngeal RT-PCR tests are negative.
items.reason: Only cases of true encephalitis caused by viral CNS invasion have a positive CSF RT-PCR for SARS-CoV-2.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; nasopharyngeal RT-PCR tests are positive in both encephalopathy and encephalitis cases, but only encephalitis has a positive CSF RT-PCR.

items.assertion: The presence of CSF WNV IgM antibodies is diagnostic of WNV encephalitis.
items.reason: IgM antibodies have a high molecular weight, restricting their passage from serum to CSF through the blood-brain barrier.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the restricted passage of IgM supports its diagnostic value for WNV encephalitis.

items.assertion: HSV CSF antibody testing is useful in acute diagnosis.
items.reason: Antibody synthesis occurs immediately after infection onset.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because optimal detection of HSV antibodies occurs after the first week, not immediately.

items.subtopic: CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis
items.assertion: Patients with VZV encephalitis may show multifocal areas of hemorrhagic and ischemic infarction.
items.reason: This reflects the tendency of VZV to produce a CNS vasculopathy rather than true encephalitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with VZV encephalitis may show these MRI findings.
items.reference: Encephalitis · CSF POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION (part 3) | TABLE 142-2 Use of Diagnostic Tests in Encephalitis

items.assertion: HSV encephalitis should be suspected in patients with focal findings on neuroimaging studies.
items.reason: Focal findings on clinical examination, neuroimaging studies, or EEG are consistent with acute encephalitis and warrant suspicion of HSV encephalitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HSV encephalitis should be suspected.

items.assertion: Patients with WNV infection can present with acute flaccid paralysis.
items.reason: WNV infection is associated with significant involvement of deep gray matter structures, including the basal ganglia and thalamus.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why WNV can present with acute flaccid paralysis.

items.assertion: No specific therapy is available for rabies.
items.reason: Cases of rabies are almost invariably fatal with devastating neurologic sequelae in isolated survivors.
items.explanation: The lack of specific therapy contributes to the high fatality rate and severe outcomes in rabies cases.

items.assertion: Acyclovir should be infused slowly over 1 hour.
items.reason: To minimize the risk of renal dysfunction.
items.explanation: Slow infusion over 1 hour is recommended to reduce the risk of renal dysfunction.

items.assertion: Acyclovir resistance in HSV encephalitis can be mediated by changes in viral DNA polymerase.
items.reason: Resistance may also involve alterations in the viral deoxypyrimidine kinase.
items.explanation: Both viral DNA polymerase and deoxypyrimidine kinase can mediate acyclovir resistance, but the assertion only mentions one mechanism.

items.assertion: Ganciclovir treatment can lead to granulocytopenia and thrombocytopenia.
items.reason: These side effects occur in approximately 20-25% of patients, which may necessitate dose reduction or discontinuation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ganciclovir treatment can be limited.

items.assertion: Cidofovir is effective in treating human CMV CNS disease.
items.reason: Data concerning its efficacy in human CMV CNS disease are limited, though it has shown effectiveness in experimental models of murine CMV encephalitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because data on cidofovir's efficacy in human CMV CNS disease are indeed limited.

items.assertion: Patients lacking detectable JCV antibody have a high risk of developing PML.
items.reason: The risk is <0.1 case/1000 patients for those without detectable JCV antibody.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that the risk is low.

items.assertion: Extended dosing intervals of natalizumab may reduce PML risk.
items.reason: Patients with higher JCV antibody index values are at lower risk.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
